title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
short_title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.
plain_summary: This bill stops three federal agencies from banning lead ammunition and fishing tackle on federal lands and waters where hunting and fishing are allowed. The agencies that cannot make these bans are the Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Land Management, and Forest Service. There are only two exceptions to this rule. First, any bans that already exist can stay in place. Second, these agencies can create new bans only if they can prove with local data that lead use is the main reason wildlife populations are dropping in that specific area, and the state government agrees with the ban.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers on Federal Land and Water
sections.text: This section explains when government officials can and cannot ban lead ammunition and fishing tackle on federal lands and waters. The Secretary of the Interior works with the Fish and Wildlife Service and Bureau of Land Management. The Secretary of Agriculture works with the Forest Service. These officials cannot ban lead ammunition or fishing tackle on federal lands and waters that are open for hunting or fishing. They also cannot make rules about how much lead can be in ammunition or tackle used on these lands and waters. However, there are some exceptions to this rule that already exist in current regulations. There is also a special exception that allows bans in specific areas. This exception only applies if the government official can prove two things using actual field data from that specific area. First, they must show that wildlife populations are declining mainly because of lead ammunition or tackle use. Second, any ban or new rule must match the laws of the state where the federal land or water is located, or match the state fish and wildlife department policy, or be approved by that state department. When government officials use this special exception to ban lead ammunition or tackle in a specific area, they must publish a notice in the Federal Register. This notice must explain how their ban or new rule meets all the required conditions.
